_purpose: Life Pattern for the WWI-era Great Plains wheat boom and bust cycle, 1914-1932. The economic arc of families who expanded acreage during wartime high prices, overextended on mechanization debt, and were crushed when prices collapsed — setting the stage for the Dust Bowl.
display_name: The Great Plains Wheat Boom and Bust
short_description: The wheat boom and bust was an economic arc that played out across the Great Plains during and after World War I. Families who had built stable homesteads plowed up millions of acres of native grassland during the wartime price spike, mortgaging everything to buy tractors and expand. When European agriculture recovered and wheat prices crashed in 1920-21, the debt remained but the income did not. The bust destroyed thousands of farm families a full decade before the Dust Bowl — and the plowing they did during the boom destroyed the grassland that had held the soil in place for millennia.
population: Established Great Plains farm families, many of Scandinavian, German-Russian, or Anglo-American origin, who had proved up homestead claims in the 1880s-1900s. Also speculators and newcomers drawn by wartime wheat prices. Predominantly white, Protestant, family-farm operators who saw themselves as the backbone of American democracy — and whose ruin would become a national tragedy.

lifecycle_phases.description: The baseline condition before the boom. Families who proved up homestead claims in the 1880s-1900s had achieved a fragile but genuine stability. They grew wheat, raised livestock, and lived within the constraints of semi-arid climate and limited acreage. Most farms were 160-320 acres, worked with horses and hand labor. Debt levels were modest. The community institutions built during the homestead era — churches, schools, granges, mutual-aid societies — were functioning. This was the world the boom would destroy.
record_signatures: Homestead final certificates and proof-up documents
County property tax rolls showing stable assessed values
Census records (1900, 1910) documenting established farm families
Church membership rolls in rural Lutheran and Methodist congregations
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: By {year}, the {family_name} family had been on their {origin_county} homestead for {number} years — the sod house replaced by a frame dwelling, the prairie broken and yielding, the kind of hard-won stability that made a family believe the worst was behind them.

lifecycle_phases.description: World War I drove wheat prices from under $1.00 per bushel to over $2.00, peaking above $2.20 in 1917. The federal government urged farmers to plant every available acre — 'wheat will win the war' was not a slogan but a directive. Families responded by plowing up native grassland that had never been cultivated, buying tractors on credit, and expanding acreage dramatically. A family that had farmed 160 acres might expand to 640 or more. The new Fordson tractor, available from 1917, made this possible — one family could work acreage that previously required hired hands. But the tractor cost $750-$1,000, and most families borrowed to buy one. The expansion felt patriotic and profitable simultaneously, and no one questioned whether the good times would last.
record_signatures: County mortgage records showing sharp increases 1916-1920
Farm equipment liens recorded at county courthouses
USDA county crop reports showing expanded wheat acreage
Selective Service registration cards showing farm occupations
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: In {year}, wheat was selling for over two dollars a bushel and the government said plant more — so {ancestor_name} mortgaged the homestead, bought a Fordson tractor, and plowed up the buffalo grass that had held the soil since before anyone could remember.

lifecycle_phases.description: European agriculture recovered after the war. American wheat exports dropped sharply. Prices crashed from $2.20 per bushel to under $1.00 by 1921 — and stayed low. The tractor payments, land mortgages, and expansion loans remained at their wartime levels. A family that had borrowed $2,000 against $3,000 in expected wheat income now had $2,000 in debt against $1,200 in actual income. Foreclosures surged across the plains states beginning in 1921 — a full decade before the Dust Bowl. Between 1921 and 1925, rural banks failed at a rate of roughly 500 per year nationwide, most of them in the Great Plains and upper Midwest. The boom-era prosperity vanished in a single season, but the debt did not.
record_signatures: County deed books showing forced sales
Farm Bureau and Grange records documenting cooperative relief efforts
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: The wheat that had sold for two dollars a bushel during the war was selling for ninety cents in {year}, but the note on the tractor still said $750 — and the bank in {origin_county} was not interested in {ancestor_name}'s patriotism, only {pronoun_possessive} payment.

lifecycle_phases.description: Families that survived the initial price crash entered a period of grinding decline. Wheat prices remained low through the late 1920s. Drought began hitting the western plains by 1930. The grassland plowed up during the boom exposed bare soil to wind erosion. Families that had been prosperous in 1918 were desperate by 1928 and ruined by 1932. Some held on through stubbornness, family labor, and the refusal to lose the land their parents had homesteaded. Others lost everything to foreclosure and left — some to California, some to cities, some to family in the Midwest. The communities built during the homestead era began to hollow out: churches closed, schools consolidated, businesses shuttered. The Dust Bowl that followed in 1933-1936 was not the beginning of the crisis but the final chapter of one that had been building for a decade.
record_signatures: County agricultural extension reports documenting soil erosion
County population data showing steady outmigration
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: By {year}, the {family_name} farm in {origin_county} was producing less wheat on more acres than it had before the war — the soil was thinner, the prices lower, and the debt unchanged. The Dust Bowl was still coming, but the ruin had already arrived.

entry_conditions.description: Families who had successfully proved up homestead claims in the 1880s-1900s and achieved stable farming operations. The boom tempted these established families to overexpand — their very success made them vulnerable, because they had equity to mortgage and reputations that made banks willing to lend.

Newcomers drawn to the plains by wartime wheat prices, buying land at inflated values with borrowed money. These families had no homestead-era equity cushion and were the first to fail when prices crashed. Many came from urban backgrounds or eastern farms and lacked the plains survival skills of established homesteaders.

exit_conditions.description: The wheat bust weakened families financially and ecologically. The grassland they plowed up during the boom exposed soil that would blow away during the droughts of the 1930s. Families already damaged by a decade of low prices and foreclosures were finished off by dust storms and total crop failure, triggering the Dust Bowl migration to California, the Pacific Northwest, and the Midwest.

Families that survived the initial price crash but lost everything during the Great Depression. The wheat bust merged seamlessly into the Depression for plains families — the 1920s were already a depression on the Great Plains. These families adopted survival strategies including doubling up with extended family, taking relief work, and leaving the farm for wage labor in towns and cities.

Some families survived the bust through conservative management, family labor, crop diversification, and sheer stubbornness. These families held their land through the bust and the Depression, often by reducing their standard of living to near-subsistence levels. Their children, having witnessed the catastrophe of overexpansion, tended toward conservative farming practices — or left the farm entirely for military service and the GI Bill.

chain_examples: homestead_act → scandinavian_homestead_arc → wheat_boom_bust → dust_bowl → depression_survival_strategies → gi_bill_migration
homestead_act → wheat_boom_bust (survival) → stayed_and_adapted → gi_bill_migration (next generation leaves for college)
economic_panics_20c → wheat_boom_bust (speculative entry) → dust_bowl → Route 66 migration to California

daily_life.narrative: During the boom years, a wheat family's life was dominated by the urgency of expansion. Spring plowing began as soon as the frost broke, and the new tractor ran from dawn to dark breaking grassland that had never felt a blade. Summer was cultivation and anxious watching of weather. Fall was harvest — the new combine headers cutting swaths that horse-drawn binders could never have matched. Winter was for repairs, ledger-keeping, and planning next year's expansion. After the bust, the same daily rhythm continued but with a different emotional register: the tractor that had been a symbol of progress became a symbol of debt, the expanded acreage produced less per acre as the soil degraded, and every trip to town meant facing the banker.

details.description: The Fordson tractor, introduced in 1917, could plow 6-8 acres per day compared to 1-2 acres with a horse team. A family could work four times the acreage with the same labor. But the tractor cost $750-$1,000 (equivalent to a year's farm income), required gasoline and mechanical maintenance, and depreciated rapidly. Families that bought tractors on credit during the boom found themselves making payments on worn-out machines with income from a crashed market. By 1925, many families owed more on their equipment than it was worth.
details.source: Hurt, R. Douglas. 'The Great Plains During World War I.' University Press of Kansas, 2014, pp. 115-135.

details.description: The U.S. Food Administration under Herbert Hoover launched a campaign urging farmers to maximize wheat production. 'Wheat Will Win the War' posters appeared in every post office and general store. County agricultural agents pushed expansion. Families that had been prudent stewards of grassland for decades were told that plowing it up was a patriotic duty. The government-backed price guarantee of $2.20 per bushel, enacted in 1917, removed the last reason for caution. When the guarantee expired after the war, the acreage remained plowed but the price did not remain guaranteed.
details.source: Worster, Donald. 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s.' Oxford University Press, 1979, pp. 82-97.

details.description: As families lost their farms and left the plains, the community institutions they had built during the homestead era collapsed. Rural churches lost congregations. One-room schools closed as enrollment dropped below the minimum. General stores shut down as customers disappeared. The rural mail carrier's route shortened. A township that supported three churches, a school, and a store in 1918 might have one church, a consolidated school five miles away, and no store by 1928. The social fabric unraveled alongside the economic one.
details.source: Fite, Gilbert C. 'The Farmers' Frontier, 1865-1900.' University of Oklahoma Press, 1966, pp. 200-215.

economic_trajectory.narrative: The wheat boom and bust describes a classic volatile trajectory: rapid accumulation during the boom, catastrophic loss during the bust, and grinding decline thereafter. A family that had $2,000 in equity and no debt in 1914 might have $8,000 in equity and $4,000 in debt by 1919 — a net gain that looked like prosperity. By 1922, the same family had $3,000 in equity (land values had crashed with crop prices) and still $3,500 in debt — technically insolvent. The arithmetic was merciless: falling commodity prices, fixed debt obligations, and declining soil productivity created a downward spiral that no amount of hard work could reverse.

details.description: Wheat prices tell the story in numbers. In 1913, wheat averaged $0.80 per bushel. By 1917, it reached $2.20 under the federal price guarantee. After the guarantee expired, prices fluctuated around $1.50 in 1919, then crashed to $0.92 in 1921. They recovered slightly to $1.00-$1.25 in the mid-1920s, then collapsed again to $0.38 by 1932. A family that had calculated its debt payments against $2.00 wheat found itself paying those same debts with wheat worth less than a fifth of what they had planned on.
details.source: Hurt, R. Douglas. 'The Great Plains During World War I,' pp. 140-155.

details.description: The agricultural depression of the 1920s destroyed the rural banking system. Between 1921 and 1929, approximately 5,000 rural banks failed nationwide — most of them in Great Plains and upper Midwest farm communities. Each failure wiped out the savings of depositors who had no federal insurance (FDIC was not created until 1933). A family that lost its farm to foreclosure often lost its savings in the same bank failure. The concentration of failures in agricultural regions meant that the Great Depression arrived on the plains a full decade before it hit Wall Street.

Great Plains farmland values followed wheat prices downward. Land that sold for $30-$50 per acre during the boom fell to $10-$20 per acre by the mid-1920s and under $5 per acre during the Depression. A family that had mortgaged 320 acres at $40 per acre ($12,800) now owed more than the land was worth. Selling the farm would not cover the mortgage. Staying on the farm meant working land whose value was less than the debt against it. This underwater condition — familiar to later Americans through the 2008 housing crisis — trapped families between foreclosure and futility.
details.source: Fite, Gilbert C. 'The Farmers' Frontier,' pp. 218-230.

family_dynamics.narrative: The wheat boom and bust reshaped family dynamics through the lens of financial stress and broken expectations. During the boom, families expanded not just their acreage but their ambitions — building better houses, buying automobiles, planning for children's education. The bust reversed all of it. Families that had tasted prosperity returned to subsistence. Children who had expected to inherit a thriving farm watched it sold at auction. The psychological impact was devastating: fathers who had expanded during the boom bore the guilt of having risked the family's security. Mothers who had managed growing households contracted back to making do with nothing. The generational rift was profound — children who witnessed the bust often refused to farm, choosing military service, factory work, or urban professions over the land that had betrayed their parents.

details.description: Farm wives' letters and diaries from the boom-bust era frequently reveal a gendered divide in risk tolerance. Women, who managed household budgets and saw the daily costs of expansion, were often more cautious than their husbands about mortgaging for tractors and new acreage. But the decision to expand was culturally coded as a masculine domain — a man's duty to seize opportunity, to respond to the patriotic call, to prove his farming ambition. When the bust came, women bore the work of contracting the household: preserving more food, sewing instead of buying, stretching supplies that had been plentiful during the boom years.
details.source: Fink, Deborah. 'Agrarian Women: Wives and Mothers in Rural Nebraska, 1880-1940.' University of North Carolina Press, 1992, pp. 145-170.

details.description: Children who grew up during the bust witnessed their parents' humiliation at auction, their neighbors' departure, and the slow death of their communities. Many developed a deep aversion to farming. Military service during World War II provided an exit that was both economically advantageous (GI Bill benefits) and culturally acceptable (patriotic service). The result was a generational break: the homesteading grandparents built the farm, the boom-bust parents nearly lost it, and the grandchildren left it entirely. This pattern accelerated rural depopulation across the Great Plains for the rest of the twentieth century.
details.source: Hurt, R. Douglas. 'The Great Plains During World War I,' pp. 180-195.

details.item: Extended family networks as survival mechanism
details.description: Families that survived the bust often did so through extended kinship networks. Adult children who had moved to cities sent money home. Relatives doubled up on a single farm, pooling labor and reducing costs. Grandparents took in grandchildren whose parents had lost their farm. These strategies — visible in census records as multi-family households and in county records as deed transfers between relatives — preserved family land at the cost of individual independence. A family that entered the boom as a single nuclear household on its own quarter-section might survive the bust as a three-generation extended family on the same land.

record_signatures.description: County recorder offices hold mortgage filings that spike dramatically during the boom years (1916-1920) and foreclosure filings that surge during the bust (1921-1930). Farm equipment liens — recorded when a family purchased a tractor or combine on credit — document the mechanization that drove the expansion.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Establishes the family's financial trajectory with dated precision. A mortgage filed in 1917 and a foreclosure filed in 1923 tells the entire story. Equipment liens identify the specific technology the family adopted.

record_signatures.description: The U.S. Department of Agriculture published annual county-level reports on crop acreage, yields, and prices. These reports document the expansion of wheat acreage during the boom and the collapse of yields and prices during the bust at a local level.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Provides the economic context for a specific family's decisions. If county wheat acreage doubled between 1914 and 1919, the family's expansion was part of a community-wide pattern, not an individual gamble.

record_signatures.record_type: Rural bank records and receivership files
record_signatures.description: When rural banks failed (roughly 5,000 between 1921 and 1929), state banking commissions appointed receivers who filed reports listing depositors and their balances. These receivership records, held in state archives, document which families lost savings in bank failures.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Documents a family's financial loss that no other record captures. A depositor list showing a family with $500 in a failed bank reveals the erasure of years of savings in a single event.

record_signatures.description: County court records document mortgage foreclosures and the forced sales that followed. Sheriff's sale notices were published in local newspapers and record the date, property description, outstanding debt, and sale price. These records are often the last appearance of a family in the county before departure.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Documents the precise moment of loss and can identify where a family went next (the buyer's identity and the family's subsequent absence from county records).

record_signatures.description: County agricultural agents kept records of their work with local farmers, including crop demonstration results, soil conservation recommendations, and correspondence. These records, often held in state university archives, document the human dimension of the bust — farmers seeking advice, agents warning about soil depletion, and the slow recognition that the boom had caused ecological damage.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: May name specific families and document their farming practices, providing context that census and deed records cannot. Extension agent correspondence sometimes captures individual families' decisions and circumstances.

cultural_markers.marker: Mortgage-funded expansion visible in county records
cultural_markers.description: A family that appears in county records with a modest mortgage in 1914 and a much larger mortgage in 1917-1918, accompanied by equipment liens, was riding the wheat boom. The financial documents tell the story of ambition, patriotism, and overextension before any other record does.

cultural_markers.marker: Sudden disappearance from county records after foreclosure
cultural_markers.description: A family that appears consistently in census, tax, and church records through the 1910s and then vanishes from the county by 1925-1930 likely lost their farm in the bust. The absence is the evidence — and tracing where they went next is a key genealogical challenge.

Census records showing a father listed as 'farmer' and his adult children listed as 'factory worker,' 'mechanic,' or 'soldier' in the next census reveal the generational break the bust produced. Children who watched their parents lose the farm rarely chose to farm themselves.

The 1930 census frequently shows extended family members sharing a single household on the Great Plains — adult children with their families living with parents, or elderly parents taken in by children who still held land. These multi-generational households are survival structures, not cultural preferences.

narrative_hooks: The government said 'plant more wheat — it's your patriotic duty,' so {ancestor_name} plowed up the buffalo grass in {origin_county} that had held the soil since before anyone could remember — and when the price crashed, the soil blew away too.

In {year}, {ancestor_name} mortgaged the homestead to buy a tractor, because wheat at two dollars a bushel made the arithmetic irresistible. By {next_year}, wheat was under a dollar and the note on the tractor was the only number that hadn't changed.

The {family_name} family had been on their {origin_county} homestead since {year} — but the boom that was supposed to make them prosperous made them bankrupt instead, and the land their grandparents had proved up was sold at the courthouse steps.
When the bank in {origin_county} failed in {year}, it took the {family_name} family's savings with it — the money they'd set aside for exactly the kind of crisis that was now erasing their future.

{ancestor_name} watched the neighbors leave one by one — the Andersons in {year}, the Petersons the year after — until the church could not fill a pew and the school had more empty desks than children.

The bust arrived on the Great Plains a full decade before the Dust Bowl, and by the time the dust storms came, families like {ancestor_name}'s had already been fighting a losing battle with debt, drought, and deflation for ten years.

counter_narratives.myth: The Dust Bowl caused the agricultural crisis on the Great Plains.
counter_narratives.reality: The agricultural crisis on the Great Plains began in 1920-21 with the postwar wheat price collapse, a full decade before the Dust Bowl. By the time the dust storms arrived in the mid-1930s, thousands of families had already lost their farms to foreclosure and left the plains. The Dust Bowl was the final catastrophe in a sequence that began with wartime overexpansion, not the initiating event.
counter_narratives.source: Worster, Donald. 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s.' Oxford University Press, 1979, pp. 82-97.

counter_narratives.myth: Plains farmers who expanded during the boom were greedy speculators who got what they deserved.
counter_narratives.reality: Most families who expanded during the boom were responding to direct government encouragement, patriotic duty campaigns, and a guaranteed price floor. The federal government actively promoted expansion through the Food Administration, county agricultural agents, and the $2.20 price guarantee. Families were doing exactly what their government asked. The moral failure was not in the plowing but in the abandonment of those families when the prices collapsed.
counter_narratives.source: Hurt, R. Douglas. 'The Great Plains During World War I.' University Press of Kansas, 2014, pp. 115-155.

counter_narratives.myth: The Great Plains were always unsuitable for farming, and homesteaders should have known better.
counter_narratives.reality: The Great Plains supported productive wheat farming for decades under responsible management — and continue to do so today. The problem was not farming the plains but the specific combination of wartime overexpansion, debt-financed mechanization, and the plowing of marginal grassland that should never have been broken. Families that maintained conservative acreage and preserved native grass on fragile soils often survived both the bust and the Dust Bowl.
counter_narratives.source: Cunfer, Geoff. 'On the Great Plains: Agriculture and Environment.' Texas A&M University Press, 2005, pp. 55-80.

source_references: Worster, Donald. 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s.' Oxford University Press, 1979.
Hurt, R. Douglas. 'The Great Plains During World War I.' University Press of Kansas, 2014.
Fink, Deborah. 'Agrarian Women: Wives and Mothers in Rural Nebraska, 1880-1940.' University of North Carolina Press, 1992.
Fite, Gilbert C. 'The Farmers' Frontier, 1865-1900.' University of Oklahoma Press, 1966.
Cunfer, Geoff. 'On the Great Plains: Agriculture and Environment.' Texas A&M University Press, 2005.
